# Cell Surface Receptors

February 2023

![](https://www.rxmd.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/Presentation1_revised_2a-2.jpg)

Receptors can be classified in various ways. Based on their function they can be classified as either “cargo” type receptors or signal (transduction) receptors. Cargo-type receptors deliver key metabolic substrates, nutrients, and minerals to cells. In general, these receptors are present in clusters in pits and are endocytosed by the formation of vesicles, which are pinched off from the surface. Once internalized, the receptors dissociate from their ligands when they are exposed to lower pH in endosomes. Thereafter, the receptor very often folds back on itself to obtain a closed conformation and recycles to the cell surface.

Signal receptors transmit signals from the extracellular space into the intracellular area. Based on their location, these signal receptors are classified as cell surface receptors or nuclear/cytoplasmic receptors. The **cell surface receptors** can be broadly divided into G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), receptors with enzymatic activity (intrinsic or linked), and ligand-gated ion channels (LGICs).

**GPCRs** couple with G proteins in the cytosol. GPCRs are characterized by the presence of an extracellular amino terminus, an intracellular carboxy-terminal tail, and a shared structural core composed of seven transmembrane α helices that weave in and out of the membrane, thus forming three intracellular and three extracellular loops. G proteins are heterotrimeric proteins that comprise an α, β, and γ subunits. Agonist-activated GPCRs promote the dissociation of GDP bound to the α subunit of heterotrimeric G proteins and its replacement by GTP. Gα and Gβγ subunits can then activate numerous downstream effectors.

**Receptors with intrinsic/linked enzymatic activity** include four known types of receptors with intrinsic enzyme activity: Receptor tyrosine kinase (insulin receptor); Receptor-like tyrosine phosphatases (CD45 protein); Receptor serine/threonine kinases (TGF-b receptor); and Receptor guanylyl cyclases (atrial natriuretic peptide receptor); and two with linked enzymatic activity: Tyrosine kinase associated receptors (prolactin) and histidine kinase associated receptors (not seen in animal cells).

A unique feature for activation of these 6 receptor classes is ligand-induced receptor dimerization or oligomerization. As a result of the juxtaposition of the intracellular kinase domains, it allows for autophosphorylation within the complex.

Receptor tyrosine kinases are the most common and widely distributed. The majority of these are receptors for growth factors such as epidermal growth factor (EGF), platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), fibroblast growth factor (FGF), hepatocyte growth factor (HGF), nerve growth factor (NGF), and hormones such as insulin.

**LGIC** are a subclass of cell surface receptors that initiate cellular signaling by their intrinsic channel activities, which allow, upon activation by ligand binding, the rapid movement of ions across cell membranes. The acetylcholine receptor of the synapse, which has intrinsic sodium channel activity is an example of LGIC.

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# Cell surface receptor

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/PDB_1hzx_7TM_Sketch_Membrane.png/330px-PDB_1hzx_7TM_Sketch_Membrane.png)

**Cell surface receptors** (**membrane receptors**, **transmembrane receptors**) are [receptors](/wiki/Receptor_(biochemistry) "Receptor (biochemistry)") that are embedded in the [plasma membrane](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane") of [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)").[[1]](#cite_note-libretexts-1) They act in [cell signaling](/wiki/Cell_signaling "Cell signaling") by receiving (binding to) [extracellular molecules](/wiki/Extracellular_matrix "Extracellular matrix"). They are specialized [integral membrane proteins](/wiki/Integral_membrane_protein "Integral membrane protein") that allow communication between the cell and the [extracellular space](/wiki/Extracellular "Extracellular"). The extracellular molecules may be [hormones](/wiki/Hormone "Hormone"), [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter"), [cytokines](/wiki/Cytokine "Cytokine"), [growth factors](/wiki/Growth_factor "Growth factor"), [cell adhesion molecules](/wiki/Cell_adhesion_molecule "Cell adhesion molecule"), or [nutrients](/wiki/Nutrient "Nutrient"); they react with the receptor to induce changes in the [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism") and activity of a cell. In the process of [signal transduction](/wiki/Signal_transduction "Signal transduction"), [ligand binding](/wiki/Ligand_(biochemistry)#Receptor/ligand_binding_affinity "Ligand (biochemistry)") affects a [cascading chemical change](/wiki/Biochemical_cascade "Biochemical cascade") through the cell membrane.

## Structure and mechanism

Many membrane receptors are [transmembrane proteins](/wiki/Transmembrane_protein "Transmembrane protein"). There are various kinds, including [glycoproteins](/wiki/Glycoprotein "Glycoprotein") and [lipoproteins](/wiki/Lipoprotein "Lipoprotein").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Hundreds of different receptors are known and many more have yet to be studied.[[3]](#cite_note-pmid11830263-3)[[4]](#cite_note-pmid19387439-4) Transmembrane receptors are typically classified based on their [tertiary](/wiki/Biomolecular_structure#Tertiary_structure "Biomolecular structure") (three-dimensional) structure. If the three-dimensional structure is unknown, they can be classified based on [membrane topology](/wiki/Membrane_topology "Membrane topology"). In the simplest receptors, [polypeptide chains](/wiki/Protein "Protein") cross the [lipid bilayer](/wiki/Lipid_bilayer "Lipid bilayer") once, while others, such as the [G-protein coupled receptors](/wiki/G_protein-coupled_receptor "G protein-coupled receptor"), cross as many as seven times. Each [cell membrane](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane") can have several kinds of membrane receptors, with varying surface distributions. A single receptor may also be differently distributed at different membrane positions, depending on the sort of membrane and cellular function. Receptors are often clustered on the membrane surface, rather than evenly distributed.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Mechanism

Two models have been proposed to explain transmembrane receptors' mechanism of action.

## Domains

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Transmembrane_receptor.svg/330px-Transmembrane_receptor.svg.png)

Transmembrane receptors in [plasma membrane](/wiki/Plasma_membrane "Plasma membrane") can usually be divided into three parts.

### Extracellular domains

The extracellular domain is just externally from the cell or [organelle](/wiki/Organelle "Organelle"). If the polypeptide chain crosses the bilayer several times, the external domain comprises loops entwined through the membrane. By definition, a receptor's main function is to recognize and respond to a type of ligand. For example, a [neurotransmitter](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter"), [hormone](/wiki/Hormone "Hormone"), or atomic ions may each bind to the extracellular domain as a ligand coupled to receptor. [Klotho](/wiki/Klotho_(biology) "Klotho (biology)") is an enzyme which effects a receptor to recognize the ligand ([FGF23](/wiki/FGF23 "FGF23")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Transmembrane domains

Two most abundant classes of transmembrane receptors are [GPCR](/wiki/GPCR "GPCR") and [single-pass transmembrane proteins](/wiki/Single-pass_transmembrane_protein "Single-pass transmembrane protein").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) In some receptors, such as the [nicotinic acetylcholine receptor](/wiki/Acetylcholine_receptor "Acetylcholine receptor"), the transmembrane domain forms a protein pore through the membrane, or around the [ion channel](/wiki/Ion_channel "Ion channel"). Upon activation of an extracellular domain by binding of the appropriate ligand, the pore becomes accessible to ions, which then diffuse. In other receptors, the transmembrane domains undergo a conformational change upon binding, which affects intracellular conditions. In some receptors, such as members of the [7TM superfamily](/wiki/G_protein-coupled_receptor "G protein-coupled receptor"), the transmembrane domain includes a ligand binding pocket.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Intracellular domains

The intracellular (or [cytoplasmic](/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm")) domain of the receptor interacts with the interior of the cell or organelle, relaying the signal. There are two fundamental paths for this interaction:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Signal transduction

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/The_External_Reactions_and_the_Internal_Reactions.jpg/500px-The_External_Reactions_and_the_Internal_Reactions.jpg)

[Signal transduction](/wiki/Signal_transduction "Signal transduction") processes through membrane receptors involve the external reactions, in which the ligand binds to a membrane receptor, and the internal reactions, in which intracellular response is triggered.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-KSFM-11)

Signal transduction through membrane receptors requires four parts:

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Three_conformation_states_of_acetylcholine_receptor.jpg/500px-Three_conformation_states_of_acetylcholine_receptor.jpg)

Membrane receptors are mainly divided by structure and function into 3 classes: The [ion channel linked receptor](/wiki/Ion_channel_linked_receptors "Ion channel linked receptors"); The [enzyme-linked receptor](/wiki/Enzyme-linked_receptor "Enzyme-linked receptor"); and The [G protein-coupled receptor](/wiki/G_protein-coupled_receptor "G protein-coupled receptor").

### Ion channel-linked receptor

During the signal transduction event in a neuron, the neurotransmitter binds to the receptor and alters the conformation of the protein. This opens the ion channel, allowing extracellular ions into the cell. Ion permeability of the plasma membrane is altered, and this transforms the extracellular chemical signal into an intracellular electric signal which alters the [cell excitability](/wiki/Cell_excitability "Cell excitability").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The [acetylcholine receptor](/wiki/Acetylcholine_receptor "Acetylcholine receptor") is a receptor linked to a cation channel. The protein consists of four subunits: alpha (α), beta (β), gamma (γ), and delta (δ) subunits. There are two α subunits, with one [acetylcholine](/wiki/Acetylcholine "Acetylcholine") binding site each. This receptor can exist in three conformations. The closed and unoccupied state is the native protein conformation. As two molecules of acetylcholine both bind to the binding sites on α subunits, the conformation of the receptor is altered and the gate is opened, allowing for the entry of many ions and small molecules. However, this open and occupied state only lasts for a minor duration and then the gate is closed, becoming the closed and occupied state. The two molecules of acetylcholine will soon dissociate from the receptor, returning it to the native closed and unoccupied state.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Enzyme-linked receptors

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Structure_of_IGF-1R.jpg/500px-Structure_of_IGF-1R.jpg)

As of 2009, there are 6 known types of [enzyme-linked receptors](/wiki/Enzyme-linked_receptor "Enzyme-linked receptor"): Receptor [tyrosine kinases](/wiki/Tyrosine_kinase "Tyrosine kinase"); Tyrosine kinase associated receptors; Receptor-like [tyrosine phosphatases](/wiki/Protein_tyrosine_phosphatase "Protein tyrosine phosphatase"); Receptor [serine](/wiki/Serine "Serine")/[threonine](/wiki/Threonine "Threonine") [kinases](/wiki/Kinase "Kinase"); Receptor [guanylyl cyclases](/wiki/Guanylyl_cyclase "Guanylyl cyclase") and [histidine kinase](/wiki/Histidine_kinase "Histidine kinase") associated receptors. Receptor tyrosine kinases have the largest population and widest application. The majority of these molecules are receptors for [growth factors](/wiki/Growth_factor "Growth factor") such as [epidermal growth factor](/wiki/Epidermal_growth_factor "Epidermal growth factor") (EGF), [platelet-derived growth factor](/wiki/Platelet-derived_growth_factor "Platelet-derived growth factor") (PDGF), [fibroblast growth factor](/wiki/Fibroblast_growth_factor "Fibroblast growth factor") (FGF), [hepatocyte growth factor](/wiki/Hepatocyte_growth_factor "Hepatocyte growth factor") (HGF), [nerve growth factor](/wiki/Nerve_growth_factor "Nerve growth factor") (NGF) and [hormones](/wiki/Hormone "Hormone") such as [insulin](/wiki/Insulin "Insulin").
Most of these receptors will dimerize after binding with their ligands, in order to activate further signal transductions. For example, after the [epidermal growth factor (EGF)](/wiki/Epidermal_growth_factor "Epidermal growth factor") receptor binds with its ligand EGF, the two receptors dimerize and then undergo [phosphorylation](/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation") of the [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine") residues in the enzyme portion of each receptor molecule. This will activate the tyrosine kinase and catalyze further intracellular reactions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### G protein-coupled receptors

G protein-coupled receptors comprise a large [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein") family of transmembrane receptors. They are found only in [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote").[[15]](#cite_note-pmid12869759-15) The [ligands](/wiki/Ligand_(biochemistry) "Ligand (biochemistry)") which bind and activate these receptors include: photosensitive compounds, [odors](/wiki/Odor "Odor"), [pheromones](/wiki/Pheromone "Pheromone"), [hormones](/wiki/Hormone "Hormone"), and [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter"). These vary in size from small molecules to [peptides](/wiki/Peptide "Peptide") and large [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein"). G protein-coupled receptors are involved in many diseases, and thus are the targets of many modern medicinal drugs.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

There are two principal signal transduction pathways involving the G-protein coupled receptors: the [cAMP](/wiki/Cyclic_adenosine_monophosphate "Cyclic adenosine monophosphate") signaling pathway and the [phosphatidylinositol](/wiki/Phosphatidylinositol "Phosphatidylinositol") signaling pathway.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Both are mediated via [G protein](/wiki/G_protein "G protein") activation. The G-protein is a trimeric protein, with three subunits designated as α, β, and γ. In response to receptor activation, the α subunit releases bound [guanosine diphosphate](/wiki/Guanosine_diphosphate "Guanosine diphosphate") (GDP), which is displaced by [guanosine triphosphate](/wiki/Guanosine_triphosphate "Guanosine triphosphate") (GTP), thus activating the α subunit, which then dissociates from the β and γ subunits. The activated α subunit can further affect intracellular signaling proteins or target functional proteins directly.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Membrane receptor-related disease

If the membrane receptors are denatured or deficient, the signal transduction can be hindered and cause diseases. Some diseases are caused by disorders of membrane receptor function. This is due to deficiency or degradation of the receptor via changes in the genes that encode and regulate the receptor protein. The membrane receptor [TM4SF5](/wiki/TM4SF5 "TM4SF5") influences the migration of hepatic cells and [hepatoma](/wiki/Hepatoma "Hepatoma").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Also, the cortical NMDA receptor influences membrane fluidity, and is altered in Alzheimer's disease.[[19]](#cite_note-19) When the cell is infected by a non-enveloped virus, the virus first binds to specific membrane receptors and then passes itself or a subviral component to the cytoplasmic side of the cellular membrane. In the case of [poliovirus](/wiki/Poliovirus "Poliovirus"), it is known in vitro that interactions with receptors cause conformational rearrangements which release a virion protein called VP4.The N terminus of VP4 is myristylated and thus hydrophobic【[myristic acid](/wiki/Myristic_acid "Myristic acid")=CH3(CH2)12COOH】. It is proposed that the conformational changes induced by receptor binding result in the attachment of myristic acid on VP4 and the formation of a channel for RNA.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Structure-based drug design

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Flow_charts_of_two_strategies_of_structure_based_drug_design.jpg/500px-Flow_charts_of_two_strategies_of_structure_based_drug_design.jpg)

Through methods such as [X-ray crystallography](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography") and [NMR spectroscopy](/wiki/NMR_spectroscopy "NMR spectroscopy"), the information about 3D structures of target molecules has increased dramatically, and so has structural information about the ligands. This drives rapid development of [structure-based drug design](/wiki/Structure-based_drug_design "Structure-based drug design"). Some of these new drugs target membrane receptors. Current approaches to structure-based drug design can be divided into two categories. The first category is about determining ligands for a given receptor. This is usually accomplished through database queries, biophysical simulations, and the construction of chemical libraries. In each case, a large number of potential ligand molecules are screened to find those fitting the binding pocket of the receptor. This approach is usually referred to as ligand-based drug design. The key advantage of searching a database is that it saves time and power to obtain new effective compounds. Another approach of structure-based drug design is about combinatorially mapping ligands, which is referred to as receptor-based drug design. In this case, ligand molecules are engineered within the constraints of a binding pocket by assembling small pieces in a stepwise manner. These pieces can be either atoms or molecules. The key advantage of such a method is that novel structures can be discovered.[[20]](#cite_note-ligbuilder-20)[[21]](#cite_note-CBDDreview-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

--- SOURCE 5: Functions of Cell Surface Receptors ---
URL: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK9866/

SUMMARY:
All these receptors share a common structural organization: an N-terminal extracellular ligand-binding domain, a single transmembrane α helix, and a cytosolic C-terminal domain with protein-tyrosine kinase activity (Figure 13.13). The binding of ligands (e.g., growth factors) to the extracellular domains of these receptors activates their cytosolic kinase domains, resulting in phosphorylation of both the receptors themselves and intracellular target proteins that propagate the signal initiated by growth factor binding. Second, phosphorylation of tyrosine residues outside of the catalytic domain creates specific binding sites for additional proteins that transmit intracellular signals downstream of the activated receptors. Ligand binding induces receptor dimerization and leads to the activation of associated nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinases as a result of cross-phosphorylation.

![Cover of The Cell](/corehtml/pmc/pmcgifs/bookshelf/thumbs/th-cooper-lrg.png)

## The Cell: A Molecular Approach. 2nd edition.

# Functions of Cell Surface Receptors

As already reviewed, most ligands responsible for cell-cell signaling (including neurotransmitters, peptide [hormones](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3120/), and [growth factors](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3104/)) bind to receptors on the surface of their target cells. Consequently, a major challenge in understanding cell-cell signaling is unraveling the mechanisms by which cell surface receptors transmit the signals initiated by [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/) binding. As discussed in Chapter 12, some [neurotransmitter](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3198/) receptors are ligand-gated ion channels that directly control ion flux across the [plasma membrane](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3256/). Other cell surface receptors, including the receptors for peptide hormones and growth factors, act instead by regulating the activity of intracellular [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/). These proteins then transmit signals from the receptor to a series of additional intracellular targets, frequently including [transcription](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3391/) factors. Ligand binding to a receptor on the surface of the cell thus initiates a chain of intracellular reactions, ultimately reaching the target cell [nucleus](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3216/) and resulting in programmed changes in [gene](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3080/) expression. The functions of the major classes of cell surface receptors are discussed here, with the pathways of intracellular signaling downstream of these receptors being considered in the next section of this chapter.

## G Protein-Coupled Receptors

The largest family of cell surface receptors transmit signals to intracellular targets via the intermediary action of [guanine](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3105/) [nucleotide](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3214/)-binding [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) called **G proteins**. More than a thousand such [G protein-coupled receptors](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3075) have been identified, including the receptors for many neurotransmitters, neuropeptides, and peptide [hormones](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3120/). In addition, the [G protein-coupled receptor](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3075/) family includes a large number of receptors that are responsible for smell, sight, and taste.

The G protein-coupled receptors are structurally and functionally related [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) characterized by seven membrane-spanning α helices ([Figure 13.10](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2219/?report=objectonly)). The binding of ligands to the extracellular domain of these receptors induces a conformational change that allows the cytosolic domain of the receptor to bind to a G protein associated with the inner face of the [plasma membrane](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3256/). This interaction activates the G protein, which then dissociates from the receptor and carries the signal to an intracellular target, which may be either an enzyme or an [ion channel](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3137/).

![Figure 13.10. Structure of a G protein-coupled receptor.](/books/NBK9866/bin/ch13f10.gif)

#### [Figure 13.10](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2219/?report=objectonly)

Structure of a G protein-coupled receptor. The G protein-coupled receptors are characterized by seven transmembrane α helices.

The discovery of G [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) came from studies of [hormones](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3120/) (such as epinephrine) that regulate the synthesis of [cyclic AMP (cAMP)](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2999/) in their target cells. As discussed later in the chapter, cAMP is an important [second messenger](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3344/) that mediates cellular responses to a variety of hormones. In the 1970s, Martin Rodbell and his colleagues made the key observation that GTP is required for hormonal stimulation of [adenylyl cyclase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2898/) (the enzyme responsible for cAMP formation). This finding led to the discovery that a [guanine](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3105/) [nucleotide](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3214/)-binding protein (called a G protein) is an intermediary in adenylyl cyclase activation ([Figure 13.11](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2220/?report=objectonly)). Since then, an array of G proteins have been found to act as physiological switches that regulate the activities of a variety of intracellular targets in response to extracellular signals.

![Figure 13.11. Hormonal activation of adenylyl cyclase.](/books/NBK9866/bin/ch13f11.gif)

#### [Figure 13.11](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2220/?report=objectonly)

Hormonal activation of adenylyl cyclase. Binding of hormone promotes the interaction of the receptor with a G protein. The activated G protein α subunit then dissociates from the receptor and stimulates adenylyl cyclase, which catalyzes the conversion [(more...)](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2220/?report=objectonly)

G [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) consist of three subunits, designated α, β, and γ ([Figure 13.12](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2221/?report=objectonly)). They are frequently called **heterotrimeric G proteins** to distinguish them from other [guanine](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3105/) [nucleotide](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3214/)-binding proteins, such as the [Ras](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3308/) proteins discussed later in the chapter. The α subunit binds guanine nucleotides, which regulate G protein activity. In the resting state, α is bound to GDP in a complex with β and γ. Hormone binding induces a conformational change in the receptor, such that the cytosolic domain of the receptor interacts with the G protein and stimulates the release of bound GDP and its exchange for GTP. The activated GTP-bound α subunit then dissociates from β and γ, which remain together and function as a βγ complex. Both the active GTP-bound α subunit and the βγ complex then interact with their targets to elicit an intracellular response. The activity of the α subunit is terminated by hydrolysis of the bound GTP, and the inactive α subunit (now with GDP bound) then reassociates with the βγ complex, ready for the cycle to start anew.

![Figure 13.12. Regulation of G proteins.](/books/NBK9866/bin/ch13f12.gif)

#### [Figure 13.12](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2221/?report=objectonly)

Regulation of G proteins.

Mammalian genomes encode at least 20 different α subunits, 6 β subunits, and 12 γ subunits. Different G [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) associate with different receptors, so this array of G proteins couples receptors to distinct intracellular targets. For example, the G protein associated with the epinephrine receptor is called Gs because its α subunit *s*timulates [adenylyl cyclase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2898/) (see [Figure 13.11](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2220/?report=objectonly)). Other G protein α and βγ subunits act instead to inhibit adenylyl cyclase or to regulate the activities of other target [enzymes](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3042/).

In addition to regulating target [enzymes](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3042/), both the α and βγ subunits of some G [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) directly regulate ion channels. A good example is provided by the action of the [neurotransmitter](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3198/) acetylcholine on heart muscle, which is distinct from its effects on nerve and skeletal muscle. The acetylcholine receptor on nerve and skeletal muscle cells is a [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/)-gated [ion channel](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3137/) (see [Figure 12.23](/books/n/cooper/A1986/figure/A1999/?report=objectonly)). Heart muscle cells have a different acetylcholine receptor, which is G protein-coupled. This G protein is designated Gi because its α subunit *i*nhibits [adenylyl cyclase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2898/). In addition, the Gi βγ subunits act directly to open K+ channels in the [plasma membrane](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3256/), which has the effect of slowing heart muscle contraction.

## Receptor Protein-Tyrosine Kinases

In contrast to the G protein-coupled receptors, other cell surface receptors are directly linked to intracellular [enzymes](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3042/). The largest family of such enzyme-linked receptors are the [receptor protein-tyrosine kinases](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3310), which phosphorylate their [substrate](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3369/) [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) on tyrosine residues. This family includes the receptors for most [polypeptide](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3269/) [growth factors](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3104/), so protein-tyrosine [phosphorylation](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3251/) has been particularly well studied as a signaling mechanism involved in the control of animal cell growth and differentiation. Indeed, the first [protein-tyrosine kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3295/) was discovered in 1980 during studies of the oncogenic proteins of animal [tumor](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3407/) viruses, in particular [Rous sarcoma virus](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3335/), by Tony Hunter and Bartholomew Sefton. The [EGF](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3044/) receptor was then found to function as a protein-tyrosine kinase by Stanley Cohen and his colleagues, clearly establishing protein-tyrosine phosphorylation as a key signaling mechanism in the response of cells to growth factor stimulation.

By now more than 50 receptor protein-tyrosine kinases have been identified, including the receptors for [EGF](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3044/), NGF, [PDGF](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3261/), insulin, and many other [growth factors](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3104/). All these receptors share a common structural organization: an N-terminal extracellular [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/)-binding domain, a single transmembrane [α helix](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2887/), and a cytosolic C-terminal domain with [protein-tyrosine kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3295/) activity ([Figure 13.13](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2223/?report=objectonly)). Most of the receptor protein-tyrosine kinases consist of single polypeptides, although the insulin receptor and some related receptors are dimers consisting of two pairs of [polypeptide](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3269/) chains. The binding of ligands (e.g., growth factors) to the extracellular [domains](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3021/) of these receptors activates their cytosolic kinase domains, resulting in [phosphorylation](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3251/) of both the receptors themselves and intracellular target [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) that propagate the signal initiated by growth factor binding.

![Figure 13.13. Organization of receptor protein-tyrosine kinases.](/books/NBK9866/bin/ch13f13.gif)

#### [Figure 13.13](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2223/?report=objectonly)

Organization of receptor protein-tyrosine kinases. Each receptor consists of an N-terminal extracellular ligand-binding domain, a single transmembrane α helix, and a cytosolic C-terminal domain with protein-tyrosine kinase activity. The structures [(more...)](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2223/?report=objectonly)

The first step in signaling from most receptor protein-tyrosine kinases is [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/)-induced receptor dimerization ([Figure 13.14](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2224/?report=objectonly)). Some [growth factors](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3104/), such as [PDGF](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3261/) and NGF, are themselves dimers consisting of two identical [polypeptide](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3269/) chains; these growth factors directly induce dimerization by simultaneously binding to two different receptor molecules. Other growth factors (such as [EGF](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3044/)) are monomers but have two distinct receptor binding sites that serve to crosslink receptors.

![Figure 13.14. Dimerization and autophosphorylation of receptor protein-tyrosine kinases.](/books/NBK9866/bin/ch13f14.gif)

#### [Figure 13.14](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2224/?report=objectonly)

Dimerization and autophosphorylation of receptor protein-tyrosine kinases. Growth factor binding induces receptor dimerization, which results in receptor autophosphorylation as the two polypeptide chains phosphorylate one another.

Ligand-induced dimerization then leads to **autophosphorylation** of the receptor as the dimerized [polypeptide](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3269/) chains cross-phosphorylate one another (see [Figure 13.14](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2224/?report=objectonly)). Such autophosphorylation plays two key roles in signaling from these receptors. First, [phosphorylation](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3251/) of tyrosine residues within the catalytic domain may play a regulatory role by increasing receptor [protein kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3290/) activity. Second, phosphorylation of tyrosine residues outside of the catalytic domain creates specific binding sites for additional [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) that transmit intracellular signals downstream of the activated receptors.

The association of these downstream signaling molecules with receptor protein-tyrosine kinases is mediated by protein [domains](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3021/) that bind to specific phosphotyrosine-containing peptides ([Figure 13.15](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2225/?report=objectonly)). The best-characterized of these domains are called [SH2 domains](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3349) (for *S*rc *h*omology *2*) because they were first recognized in protein-tyrosine kinases related to [Src](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3361/), the oncogenic protein of [Rous sarcoma virus](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3335/). SH2 domains consist of approximately a hundred amino acids and bind to specific short peptide sequences containing phosphotyrosine residues ([Figure 13.16](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2226/?report=objectonly)). The resulting association of SH2-containing [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) with activated receptor protein-tyrosine kinases can have several effects: It localizes the SH2-containing proteins to the [plasma membrane](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3256/), leads to their association with other proteins, promotes their [phosphorylation](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3251/), and stimulates their enzymatic activities. The association of these proteins with autophosphorylated receptors thus represents the first step in the intracellular transmission of signals initiated by the binding of [growth factors](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3104/) to the cell surface.

![Figure 13.15. Association of downstream signaling molecules with receptor protein-tyrosine kinases.](/books/NBK9866/bin/ch13f15.gif)

#### [Figure 13.15](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2225/?report=objectonly)

Association of downstream signaling molecules with receptor protein-tyrosine kinases. SH2 domains bind to specific phosphotyrosine-containing peptides of the activated receptors.

![Figure 13.16. Complex between an SH2 domain and a phosphotyrosine peptide.](/books/NBK9866/bin/ch13f16.gif)

#### [Figure 13.16](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2226/?report=objectonly)

Complex between an SH2 domain and a phosphotyrosine peptide. The polypeptide chain of the Src SH2 domain is shown in red with its surface indicated by green dots. Purple spheres indicate a groove on the surface. The three amino acid residues that interact [(more...)](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2226/?report=objectonly)

## Cytokine Receptors and Nonreceptor Protein-Tyrosine Kinases

Rather than possessing intrinsic enzymatic activity, many receptors act by stimulating intracellular protein-tyrosine kinases with which they are noncovalently associated. This family of receptors (called the **cytokine receptor superfamily**) includes the receptors for most [cytokines](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3005/) (e.g., interleukin-2 and erythropoietin) and for some [polypeptide](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3269/) [hormones](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3120/) (e.g., growth hormone). Like receptor protein-tyrosine kinases, the cytokine receptors contain N-terminal extracellular [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/)-binding [domains](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3021/), single transmembrane α helices, and C-terminal cytosolic domains. However, the cytosolic domains of the cytokine receptors are devoid of any known catalytic activity. Instead, the cytokine receptors function in association with [nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinases](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3202), which are activated as a result of ligand binding.

The first step in signaling from cytokine receptors is thought to be [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/)-induced receptor dimerization and cross-[phosphorylation](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3251/) of the associated nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinases ([Figure 13.17](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2228/?report=objectonly)). These activated kinases then phosphorylate the receptor, providing phosphotyrosine-binding sites for the recruitment of downstream signaling molecules that contain SH2 [domains](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3021/). Combinations of cytokine receptors plus associated nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinases thus function analogously to the receptor protein-tyrosine kinases discussed in the previous section.

![Figure 13.17. Signaling from cytokine receptors.](/books/NBK9866/bin/ch13f17.gif)

#### [Figure 13.17](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2228/?report=objectonly)

Signaling from cytokine receptors. Ligand binding induces receptor dimerization and leads to the activation of associated nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinases as a result of cross-phosphorylation. The activated kinases then phosphorylate tyrosine residues [(more...)](/books/NBK9866/figure/A2228/?report=objectonly)

The nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinases associated with the cytokine receptors fall into two major families. Many of these kinases are members of the [Src](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3361) family, which consists of [Src](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3361/) and eight closely related [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/). As already noted, Src was initially identified as the oncogenic protein of [Rous sarcoma virus](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3335/) and was the first protein shown to possess [protein-tyrosine kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3295/) activity, so it has played a pivotal role in experiments leading to our current understanding of cell signaling. In addition to Src family members, the cytokine receptors are associated with nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinases belonging to the [Janus kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3139), or **[JAK](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3139/)**, family. Members of the JAK family appear to be universally required for signaling from cytokine receptors, indicating that JAK family kinases play a critical role in coupling these receptors to the tyrosine [phosphorylation](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3251/) of intracellular targets. In contrast, members of the Src family play key roles in signaling from [antigen](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2914/) receptors on B and T lymphocytes but do not appear to be required for signaling from most cytokine receptors.

## Receptors Linked to Other Enzymatic Activities

Although the vast majority of enzyme-linked receptors stimulate protein-tyrosine [phosphorylation](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3251/), some receptors are associated with other enzymatic activities. These receptors include protein-tyrosine phosphatases, protein-serine/threonine kinases, and guanylyl cyclases. The functions of most of these receptors are less well understood than those of either the G protein-coupled receptors or the receptors associated with [protein-tyrosine kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3295/) activity.

[Protein-tyrosine phosphatases](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3296) remove phosphate groups from phosphotyrosine residues, thus acting to counterbalance the effects of protein-tyrosine kinases. In many cases, protein-tyrosine phosphatases play negative regulatory roles in cell signaling pathways by terminating the signals initiated by protein-tyrosine [phosphorylation](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3251/). However, some protein-tyrosine phosphatases are cell surface receptors whose enzymatic activities play a positive role in cell signaling. A good example is provided by a receptor called CD45, which is expressed on the surface of T and B lymphocytes. Following [antigen](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2914/) stimulation, CD45 is thought to dephosphorylate a specific phosphotyrosine that inhibits the enzymatic activity of [Src](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3361/) family members. Thus, the CD45 [protein-tyrosine phosphatase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3296/) acts (somewhat paradoxically) to stimulate nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinases.

The receptors for [transforming growth factor β](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3397) (**TGF-β**) and related polypeptides are protein kinases that phosphorylate serine or threonine, rather than tyrosine, residues on their [substrate](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3369/) [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/). TGF-β is the prototype of a family of [polypeptide](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3269/) [growth factors](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3104/) that control proliferation and differentiation of a variety of cell types, generally inhibiting proliferation of their target cells. The cloning of the first receptor for a member of the TGF-β family in 1991 revealed that it is the prototype of a unique receptor family with a cytosolic [protein-serine/threonine kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3294) domain. Since then, receptors for additional TGF-β family members have similarly been found to be protein-serine/threonine kinases. The binding of [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/) to these receptors results in the association of two distinct polypeptide chains, which are encoded by different members of the TGF-β receptor family, to form heterodimers in which the receptor kinases cross-phosphorylate one another. The activated TGF-β receptors then phosphorylate members of a family of [transcription](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3391/) factors called SMADs, which translocate to the [nucleus](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3216/) and stimulate expression of target genes.

Some peptide ligands bind to receptors whose cytosolic [domains](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3021/) are guanylyl cyclases, which catalyze formation of cyclic GMP. As discussed earlier, nitric oxide also acts by stimulating [guanylyl cyclase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3106/), but the target of nitric oxide is an intracellular enzyme rather than a transmembrane receptor. The receptor [guanylyl cyclases](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3106) have an extracellular [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/)-binding domain, a single transmembrane [α helix](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2887/), and a cytosolic domain with catalytic activity. Ligand binding stimulates cyclase activity, leading to the formation of cyclic GMP—a [second messenger](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3344/) whose intracellular effects are discussed in the next section of this chapter.

Other receptors bind to cytoplasmic [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) with additional biochemical activities. For example, the cytokine [tumor necrosis factor](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3408/) (TNF) induces cell death, perhaps (as discussed later in this chapter) as a way of eliminating damaged or unwanted cells from tissues. The receptors for TNF and related death-signaling molecules are associated with specific proteases, which are activated in response to [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/) binding. Activation of these receptor-associated proteases triggers the activation of additional downstream proteases, ultimately leading to degradation of a variety of intracellular proteins and death of the cell.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Cell-surface receptors are integral membrane proteins that mediate communication between cells and their external environment, responding to various ligands like hormones and neurotransmitters.
Intracellular receptors are located within the cell and interact with ligands that can cross the plasma membrane, such as steroid hormones, initiating gene expression changes.
Receptors play a crucial role in signal transduction by converting extracellular signals into intracellular responses, activating cascades that influence cellular functions and gene expression.
